critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression hospital readmission design has a critical test set contamination issue explicitly written into the protocol and a split design problem.

Section 6 explicitly states: 'a final check on a subset of the test set is performed to ensure the chosen model configuration has an advantage before committing to full training. If the test subset indicates validation overfitting, slight adjustments are made before full test evaluation.' This is not a conservative safeguard — it is described as an adaptive procedure where model configuration adjustments are made after observing test subset performance. This constitutes iterative test set use and violates the basic holdout protocol. Any reported test set metrics are optimistic because the configuration was adjusted to pass a preliminary test set screen.

The split uses stratified random sampling across 5 years, not a temporal split. For a dataset of patients with 'frequent healthcare utilization' — patients who have multiple admissions over 5 years — the same patient can appear in both training and test sets. The RNN processes 'the most recent 50 encounters per patient,' and if early encounters are in training while later encounters (ending near readmission) are in the test set, the model can learn patient-specific temporal patterns in training that directly transfer to predicting the test-set admission for the same patient. This is patient-level leakage in a sequential model context.

The patient selection criterion — 'primarily from patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization' — introduces significant selection bias. Patients with frequent utilization are not representative of the general hospital population and have systematically different readmission risk profiles (higher baseline risk, more comorbidities, more established care patterns). A model trained and evaluated on this subpopulation cannot support claims about readmission prediction in general. The design does not acknowledge this selection restriction as a scope limitation.

The logistic regression receives 'aggregated temporal features' (count of prior admissions, average length of stay, proportion of prior admissions resulting in readmission) while the RNN receives full sequential diagnosis/procedure/lab histories. The feature sets are not matched: the logistic regression gets explicitly constructed temporal aggregates, while the RNN gets raw sequences. This means the LR has access to a pre-computed temporal feature (proportion of prior admissions resulting in readmission) that directly encodes the label's historical frequency — a very strong predictor that the RNN must discover from raw sequences. The comparison is between a model with a hand-engineered strong predictor and a model that must learn it, which is not a fair test of temporal modeling.

F1-macro is the primary metric but early stopping uses validation F1 score 'not improving for 5 consecutive epochs.' For class-imbalanced data with ~10-15% readmission rate, F1-macro is dominated by the rare class, and early stopping at 5 consecutive epochs of no improvement is very aggressive — the model may stop before converging on the minority class.
all_issues_raised: The test set is explicitly used for preliminary configuration checks, with 'slight adjustments' made if initial results indicate validation overfitting — this adaptive test set use invalidates the holdout guarantee and makes reported metrics optimistically biased.

Stratified random splitting allows the same high-utilization patient to appear in both training and test sets; the RNN can memorize patient-specific sequential patterns during training and apply them to the same patient's later admissions at test time.

The study population is restricted to patients with frequent healthcare utilization, introducing selection bias that makes results non-representative of general hospital readmission prediction and unacknowledged in the scope statement.

The logistic regression receives a pre-computed feature encoding the historical readmission rate (proportion of prior admissions resulting in readmission) while the RNN must learn this from raw sequences — this is not a fair test of temporal modeling capacity.

Early stopping patience of 5 epochs is too aggressive for class-imbalanced binary classification where the F1-macro stopping criterion is dominated by the rare class and requires more training to converge.
all_issues_adjudicated: The test set is explicitly used for preliminary configuration checks, with 'slight adjustments' made if initial results indicate validation overfitting — this adaptive test set use invalidates the holdout guarantee and makes reported metrics optimistically biased.

Stratified random splitting allows the same high-utilization patient to appear in both training and test sets; the RNN can memorize patient-specific sequential patterns during training and apply them to the same patient's later admissions at test time.

The study population is restricted to patients with frequent healthcare utilization, introducing selection bias that makes results non-representative of general hospital readmission prediction and unacknowledged in the scope statement.

The logistic regression receives a pre-computed feature encoding the historical readmission rate (proportion of prior admissions resulting in readmission) while the RNN must learn this from raw sequences — this is not a fair test of temporal modeling capacity.

Early stopping patience of 5 epochs is too aggressive for class-imbalanced binary classification where the F1-macro stopping criterion is dominated by the rare class and requires more training to converge.
